Beneath the surface of negotiations lies a whole world other than just being able to say the right thing. Under all the verbal communication is a playing field where getting to a perfect deal includes detecting and manipulating subtle non-obvious signals. To really craft a great deal and make sure that it gets implemented, you've got to not only be cognizant of these subsurface issues, but also modify them. The most important lesson from this course we want you to remember is that yes is nothing without how. Asking calibrated how questions, knowing how, and defining how are all important tools in the negotiator's arsenal. These types of calibrated questions serve multiple functions during a negotiation. They have the special ability of keeping your counterpart engaged while thwarting any attacks or bad behavior on their part. Calibrated how questions allow you to shape the negotiating environment to suit you. Asking how is a subtle and gentle way to reject their offer. It pushes your counterpart to search for alternative solutions. As an added bonus, it may well get them to bid against themselves. You can reject someone's offer without actually using the word no. The key is to switch up how you're asking the question. With this tool, you can usually get them to lower their demands significantly and find a solution that works for you. In negotiation, you also have to keep in mind that there are influencers behind your immediate counterpart. These players behind the table on the other side have their own motivations and goals. It's frequently fatal to ignore these people. Remember, even if you're talking to the CEO, you can't ignore the incentives of the people behind the table. A great way to find these hidden needs of these players is by asking how a deal will affect everyone else and how on board they are. Another trick to remember is when trying to figure out how important your counterpart is, is paying attention to their use of pronouns. When you hear a lot of first person pronouns like I, me, and my, the real power to influence decisions lies elsewhere. On the other hand, when you hear a ton of third person pronouns, it is more likely that you're dealing with someone important who is trying to keep their options open. If their sentences and word usage seem very elaborate, they are likely to be making something up. Don't let your counterpart baffle you with BS. Remember, more words and higher complexity means an increased probability that they are telling a lie. Be suspicious of yes. Test it with the rule of three. This will help you uncover hidden objections or lies. Use labels, paraphrases, and summaries to get your counterpart to reaffirm their agreement at least three times. When employing this tactic, remember the 738-55 rule. That is, the majority of a message someone is conveying is made up of body language and tone of voice. Misalignment between the words and the nonverbal signs will reveal to you when your counterpart is lying or uncomfortable with a deal. One of the most powerful dynamics that you can introduce into any conversation is forced empathy. A really easy way to trigger this is by introducing yourself. Make sure they know your name. Stick to just your first name as much as possible because the rest quickly becomes noise. This helps you let the party view you as a real person and it humanizes you. Being pleasant and having a good sense of humor can go a long way. Make sure that you and your counterpart are enjoying yourselves. And you'll break the ice and see roadblocks vanish. And who knows, maybe you'll even get your own special discount. One final point. The art of closing a deal is staying aware to the very end. There are critical moments, especially towards the end of a negotiation, where you smell the goal so much you miss important cues. Don't get distracted by how great it will be if the deal is consummated. This is how people let themselves get taken hostage. Keep working the process. When you do this, you'll find that you can sustain a positive relationship with your counterpart while simultaneously getting outcomes that suit you.
